schools.summary: Micah, a man of mount Ephraim: 'and there was a man of mount Ephraim, whose name was Micah' — the account of Micah, whose idolatry illustrates the lawlessness of the time, begins (the and there was a man of mount Ephraim, whose name was Micah, Micah introduced, the figure, the man whose private idolatry opens the dark appendix of the book, the disorder of the age, the truth that Micah's story shows the religious confusion of the time, Judges 21:25, the figure of the household that sets up its own worship, the lawlessness of the day, the man Micah, the dark appendix, vv.6); the man of mount Ephraim (the introduction) — the place (mount Ephraim); 'whose name was Micah' (Micah named, the subject of the account, the introduction, Judges 21:25, the figure, the household that sets up its own worship, the providence, the introduction, Micah's confession of the stolen silver to follow, vv.2) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Micah confessing the stolen silver, and his mother's blessing: 'and he said unto his mother, The eleven hundred shekels of silver that were taken from thee, about which thou cursedst, and spakest of also in mine ears, behold, the silver is with me; I took it. schools.summary: The mother dedicating the silver to make an idol, in the LORD's name: 'and when he had restored the eleven hundred shekels of silver to his mother, his mother said, I had wholly dedicated the silver unto the LORD from my hand for my son, to make a graven image and a molten image: now therefore I will restore it unto thee' — the mother dedicated the silver to the LORD, yet to make a forbidden image (the and when he had restored the eleven hundred shekels of silver to his mother, the silver restored, the his mother said, I had wholly dedicated the silver unto the LORD from my hand for my son, to make a graven image and a molten image: now therefore I will restore it unto thee, dedicated to an idol, the figure, the silver dedicated to the LORD yet to a graven image, the worship corrupted, the truth that the mother dedicated the silver to the LORD but to make an idol forbidden, Exodus 20:4, the figure of the will-worship that honours God in name while breaking his command, the dedication, the corrupted worship, vv.2); the silver restored (the return) — the restoring (the silver restored); 'I had wholly dedicated the silver unto the LORD... to make a graven image and a molten image' (the silver dedicated to the LORD yet to an idol, the corrupted vow, Exodus 20:4, the figure, the will-worship that honours God in name while breaking his command, the providence, the dedication, the image set up in Micah's house to follow, vv.4-5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The image made of the silver and set in Micah's house: 'yet he restored the money unto his mother; and his mother took two hundred shekels of silver, and gave them to the founder, who made thereof a graven image and a molten image: and they were in the house of Micah' — the silver was cast into an idol for Micah's house (the yet he restored the money unto his mother; and his mother took two hundred shekels of silver, and gave them to the founder, the silver to the founder, the who made thereof a graven image and a molten image: and they were in the house of Micah, the image made, the figure, the idol made and housed, the forbidden worship established, the truth that the silver became a graven and molten image in Micah's house, Deuteronomy 27:15, the figure of the idolatry set up in the household, the forbidden image enshrined, the image made, the idol housed, vv.3); the silver given to the founder (the making, Deuteronomy 27:15) — the silver (to the founder); 'made thereof a graven image and a molten image: and they were in the house of Micah' (the idol made and housed, the forbidden worship, the figure, the idolatry set up in the household, the providence, the image, Micah's house of gods and ephod to follow, vv.5) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Micah's house of gods, ephod, teraphim, and self-made priest: 'and the man Micah had an house of gods, and made an ephod, and teraphim, and consecrated one of his sons, who became his priest' — Micah set up a whole apparatus of false worship (the and the man Micah had an house of gods, and made an ephod, and teraphim, the house of gods, the and consecrated one of his sons, who became his priest, the self-made priest, the figure, the whole false worship devised by man, priest and all, against God's order, the truth that Micah made his own shrine, ephod, and priest, against the law, Exodus 28:1, the figure of the will-worship that invents its own priesthood and ritual, the house of gods, the self-made priest, vv.4); Micah's house of gods, ephod, and teraphim (the will-worship, Exodus 28:1) — the apparatus (house of gods, ephod, teraphim); 'consecrated one of his sons, who became his priest' (Micah making his own son a priest, against God's order, the usurped priesthood, the figure, the will-worship that invents its own priesthood, the providence, the will-worship, the refrain of no king in Israel to follow, vv.6) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The refrain of the book, that in those days there was no king in Israel and every man did what was right in his own eyes: 'in those days there was no king in Israel, but every man did that which was right in his own eyes' — the disorder of self-will — 'in those days there was no king in Israel, but every man did that which was right in his own eyes': the writer explains the idolatry and confusion by the want of a ruling authority and the rule of every man's own judgment (the witnesses noting the significance — the key that unlocks the whole dark appendix of Judges, the religious and moral anarchy of an age in which there was no settled authority, civil or spiritual, to restrain the people, so that each man followed his own corrupt judgment for his guide, doing not what God commanded but what seemed right to himself, the root of Micah's idolatry and of all the disorders that follow, the want of a king the want of a restraining and ordering authority, every man his own law, self-will enthroned in the place of God's word, the lesson — that when men cast off the authority of God and follow their own judgment, the issue is confusion and every evil work, 'there is a way which seemeth right unto a man, but the end thereof are the ways of death,' Proverbs 14:12, the truth that the heart of fallen man, left to itself, will not choose God's way but its own, and that self-will is the spring of all disorder, 'we have turned every one to his own way,' Isaiah 53:6, the figure — the anarchy of the soul and the society that owns no king but self, fulfilled in every age that casts off God's rule and makes private judgment the measure of right, the opposite of the kingdom where Christ is owned as King, 'we will not have this man to reign over us,' Luke 19:14, the truth that the only cure for the disorder of self-will is the acknowledged rule of God, the figure of the anarchy of self-will, the disorder that follows when every man is a law to himself, the call to submit our judgment to the rule of God, the warning that to do what is right in our own eyes is the way of ruin, the figure of the anarchy of self-will, the spiritual and civil disorder that follows when every man is a law to himself); the want of a settled ruling authority, civil and spiritual, to restrain the people, the root of the disorder of the age (the want of a king, Proverbs 14:12) — the want (no king in Israel); 'but every man did that which was right in his own eyes' (every man following his own corrupt judgment instead of God's command, self-will enthroned in the place of God's word, the heart that turns to its own way, the spring of all disorder, the rule of self-will, Isaiah 53:6; Luke 19:14) — the figure (the anarchy of the soul and society that owns no king but self, the providence, the refrain, the coming of the Levite to Micah to follow, vv.7) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'every man did that which was right in his own eyes' explain?

sides.position: The anarchy of self-will, the spiritual and civil disorder that follows when every man is a law to himself - the key to the dark appendix, the moral anarchy of an age with no settled authority to restrain, each man following his own corrupt judgment instead of God's command, the issue of casting off God's authority being confusion and every evil (Proverbs 14:12), the heart turning to its own way (Isaiah 53:6), the soul that owns no king but self (Luke 19:14)

themes: In those days there was no king in Israel, but every man did that which was right in his own eyes

the key to the whole dark appendix, the religious and moral anarchy of an age with no settled authority to restrain, each man following his own corrupt judgment, doing not what God commanded but what seemed right to himself
the want of a king the want of a restraining authority, self-will enthroned in the place of God's word, the heart of fallen man turning to its own way (Proverbs 14:12; Isaiah 53:6; Luke 19:14)

schools.summary: A young Levite of Bethlehemjudah sojourning: 'and there was a young man out of Bethlehemjudah of the family of Judah, who was a Levite, and he sojourned there' — a wandering Levite, who would become Micah's priest, is introduced (the and there was a young man out of Bethlehemjudah of the family of Judah, who was a Levite, the young Levite, the and he sojourned there, his sojourning, the figure, the wandering Levite without a settled place, the disorder of the priesthood in that day, the truth that the Levite wandered for want of a settled portion, the Levitical order neglected, Deuteronomy 12:19, the figure of the minister adrift in a disordered age, seeking a place, the young Levite, the wandering minister, vv.6); the young Levite of Bethlehem (the introduction, Deuteronomy 12:19) — the Levite (young man); 'he sojourned there' (the Levite wandering for want of a place, the disorder, the figure, the minister adrift in a disordered age, the providence, the sojourning, the Levite coming to Micah's house to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The Levite coming to Micah's house in mount Ephraim: 'and the man departed out of the city from Bethlehemjudah to sojourn where he could find a place: and he came to mount Ephraim to the house of Micah, as he journeyed' — the wandering Levite came to Micah's house (the and the man departed out of the city from Bethlehemjudah to sojourn where he could find a place, the departure, the and he came to mount Ephraim to the house of Micah, as he journeyed, the arrival, the figure, the wandering minister arriving where idolatry awaited, the providence and the snare, the truth that the Levite came to Micah's idolatrous house seeking a place, Judges 17:5, the figure of the minister who, seeking a living, falls into the service of false worship, the coming of the Levite, the arrival at Micah's, vv.7); the Levite departing to find a place (the wandering, Judges 17:5) — the departure (to find a place); 'he came to mount Ephraim to the house of Micah' (the Levite arriving at Micah's house, the providence, the figure, the minister who falls into the service of false worship, the providence, the arrival, Micah hiring the Levite to follow, vv.9-10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Micah asking the Levite whence he came: 'and Micah said unto him, Whence comest thou? And he said unto him, I am a Levite of Bethlehemjudah, and I go to sojourn where I may find a place' — the Levite told Micah his wandering state (the and Micah said unto him, Whence comest thou?, the question, the And he said unto him, I am a Levite of Bethlehemjudah, and I go to sojourn where I may find a place, the answer, the figure, the wandering Levite owning his want of a place, the opening for the snare, the truth that the Levite, seeking a place, was ripe for Micah's offer, Judges 17:8, the figure of the minister adrift, open to the temptation of an easy living, the inquiry of Micah, the wandering owned, vv.8); Micah asking the Levite's origin (the inquiry) — the question (whence comest thou); 'I am a Levite of Bethlehemjudah, and I go to sojourn where I may find a place' (the Levite owning his wandering state, the opening, Judges 17:8, the figure, the minister adrift, open to the temptation of an easy living, the providence, the inquiry, Micah's offer to follow, vv.10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Micah hiring the Levite to be his priest for wages: 'and Micah said unto him, Dwell with me, and be unto me a father and a priest, and I will give thee ten shekels of silver by the year, and a suit of apparel, and thy victuals. So the Levite went in' — Micah hired the Levite as his household priest (the and Micah said unto him, Dwell with me, and be unto me a father and a priest, the offer, the and I will give thee ten shekels of silver by the year, and a suit of apparel, and thy victuals. schools.summary: The Levite content to dwell with Micah as one of his sons: 'and the Levite was content to dwell with the man; and the young man was unto him as one of his sons' — the Levite settled contentedly into Micah's idolatrous service (the and the Levite was content to dwell with the man, the contentment, the and the young man was unto him as one of his sons, treated as a son, the figure, the minister content in the service of false worship for an easy living, the truth that the Levite was content to serve Micah's idolatry for his keep, Philippians 3:19, the figure of the hireling content in a corrupt service, the conscience silenced by ease, the contentment of the Levite, the idolatrous service, vv.10); the Levite content in Micah's service (the ease, Philippians 3:19) — the contentment (content to dwell); 'the young man was unto him as one of his sons' (the Levite treated as a son, the kindness, the figure, the hireling content in a corrupt service, the providence, the contentment, Micah's false confidence to follow, vv.12-13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Micah's false confidence, that the LORD would bless him because he had a Levite for his priest: 'then said Micah, Now know I that the LORD will do me good, seeing I have a Levite to my priest' — the false confidence in form — 'then said Micah, Now know I that the LORD will do me good, seeing I have a Levite to my priest': Micah persuades himself of God's favour because he has secured a Levite, though his whole worship is idolatrous (the witnesses noting the significance — the self-deception of a man who, in the midst of plain idolatry, his graven image, his ephod, his teraphim, his self-made shrine, yet flatters himself that he has secured God's blessing because he has procured a Levite, as if a single point of outward conformity could sanctify a worship that broke God's command at every turn, the conscience quieted by a form while the substance was abomination, the Levite a fig-leaf over the idolatry, the confidence resting on the office, not on obedience, the man persuading himself of favour while provoking God, the lesson — that men deceive themselves by resting on outward forms and observances while the heart and the worship are corrupt, presuming on God's blessing where they have no warrant, 'this people draweth nigh unto me with their mouth... but their heart is far from me,' Matthew 15:8, the truth that no correctness of form can sanctify a false worship, and that to trust in the outward while disobeying God is vain, 'will ye steal, murder... and come and stand before me in this house... and say, We are delivered?' Jeremiah 7:9-10, the figure — the false peace of formal religion, the confidence built on observances that God never owns, fulfilled in every soul that rests on the form of godliness while denying its power, 'they trusted in themselves that they were righteous,' Luke 18:9, the truth that God is not pleased by the form but by the obedient heart, and self-flattering confidence in outward religion is delusion, the figure of the false confidence that rests on outward form while the heart and worship are corrupt, the call to seek God's favour in obedience and not in forms, the warning that a religion of mere form deceives the soul to its ruin); Micah persuading himself of God's favour and blessing in the midst of his idolatry, the conscience quieted by a form (the false confidence, Matthew 15:8) — the delusion (now know I the LORD will do me good); 'seeing I have a Levite to my priest' (Micah resting his confidence on having a Levite, a single point of outward conformity, as if it could sanctify an idolatrous worship, the false peace of formal religion that God never owns, the form without obedience, Jeremiah 7:9-10; Luke 18:9) — the figure (the false peace of formal religion, the confidence built on observances God never owns, the providence, the delusion, the Danites coming to seize the idol and the priest to follow, ch.18) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Micah's confidence 'now know I that the LORD will do me good' reveal?

sides.position: The false confidence that rests on outward form and observance while the heart and the worship are corrupt - the self-deception of a man who in the midst of plain idolatry flatters himself he has secured God's blessing because he has a Levite, as if a point of outward conformity could sanctify a false worship, men resting on forms while the heart is corrupt (Matthew 15:8), no form sanctifying a false worship (Jeremiah 7:9-10), the false peace of formal religion God never owns (Luke 18:9)
